Materials Student guide About TDAA
For instructors · How to run the course

Running a TDAA course.

A step-by-step guide to running a TDAA course week to week: what the course needs before week 1, how a single week actually unfolds, the review habit that matters most, and the risks worth watching. Stays close to what was used in the original DSAA 3071 run.

This guide vs. the setup guide

This page is about running the course. For the one-time machine setup — install, fork, bootstrap, generate, build, publish, grade — see the harness setup guide. Both pages start from About TDAA.

In this guide
  1. 01 What the course needs before week 1
  2. 02 Preparing the course
  3. 03 Running a week
  4. 04 The review habit
  5. 05 Risks to watch
  6. 06 Reusing the materials and harness
§ 01

What the course needs before week 1

An instructor needs a stable source for the course content — a textbook or a complete set of notes — and a week-by-week schedule that maps content onto weeks. A way to run closed-book tests, a way to mark them quickly, and enough teaching staff to move between groups during self-paced study are also part of the operational picture.

The subject itself helps or hurts. Topics with precise definitions and short proof tasks — like Theory of Computation, discrete math, or formal methods — align naturally with weekly closed-book tests. Subjects whose evidence of understanding is essay-length or project-shaped need a different gate.

The structural requirement

Beyond the subject and the schedule, the instructor needs a harness: a controlled place to store, review, revise, and rebuild weekly materials, with named AI-assisted skills for drafting, reviewing, and grading. Without it, twelve weekly bundles and twelve graded tests are not realistic for one person.

§ 02

Preparing the course

For a new course, start from a clean starter folder rather than copying any single reference course directly. The starter contains the document style, the weekly folder structure, build commands, and the instructions given to AI assistants when they draft and review materials. Fill in the course title, textbook, instructor information, week count, and schedule — that's the /bootstrap step.

Before generating the first week, read the textbook extraction and the proposed schedule carefully and correct them by hand. The schedule is the contract with students. If a topic is not scheduled or not in the learning sheet, the test must not silently introduce it.

§ 03

Running a week

Each teaching week has four steps:

  1. Create the weekly bundle

    Run /generate-week N to produce the learning sheet, the test, the alternative test B, and the validation set in one pass. Smaller skills (/write-learning-sheet, /revise, /write-tests) give finer control when needed.

  2. Review as an instructor

    Read the bundle yourself with attention to definitions, examples, proof steps, and the test target. This is the non-negotiable step — see § 04.

  3. Publish the student-facing site

    Run make build locally to compile PDFs and viewer pages, then commit and push. The included Pages workflow redeploys the site automatically.

  4. Grade and return tests quickly

    Fast enough that feedback helps the next learning cycle. Use /grade-homework and /homework-report for AI-assisted first-pass marking; review flagged items before posting.

A representative classroom rhythm

The DSAA 3071 run used three contact hours per week:

  • 0:00 – 0:30 — Closed-book test on the previous week's material.
  • 0:30 – 2:30 — Self-paced study block. Students work in groups of about four; the instructor and TAs circulate and surface confusion. When a misconception is widespread, the instructor pauses for a short whole-room explanation.
  • 2:30 – 2:50 — Discussion segment on conceptually heavy points or upcoming applications.

The new week's validation sheet is released immediately after the test — while the gap is fresh — with its answers two days later. That cadence is part of the design, not a detail.

§ 04

The review habit

The review step is the most important technical habit in a TDAA course. AI can draft and mark quickly, but the instructor must still ask:

  • Is the explanation in the learning sheet correct?
  • Does it match the textbook's notation?
  • Does the validation sheet actually prepare students for the test?
  • Does the test only ask about prepared concepts — never a term introduced for the first time on test day?
  • Have doubtful grading decisions been flagged for human review rather than guessed?
Nothing ships unreviewed

AI operates only on the production side. The instructor is the final approver on every learning sheet, test, and grade that reaches a student.

§ 05

Risks to watch

The DSAA 3071 evidence surfaces a small set of risks. None of them break the model; all of them deserve direct attention rather than assumption.

  • Narrow practice. Students may treat the validation sheet as a complete blueprint and practice too narrowly from it. Vary phrasing and surface form between validation and test while keeping the cognitive target the same.
  • Spiky difficulty. Some weeks will feel too hard. Calibrate against prior weeks and adjust the test's point cap or worked-example density if a week lands badly.
  • Test pressure. Weekly testing creates real, recurring pressure. The 100-of-130 cap and the validation set are the design's livability features — keep them in place.
  • Confident but false AI reasoning. AI-written materials and AI-assisted marking can contain assertions that sound right and aren't. This is what the review habit (§ 04) exists to catch.
§ 06

Reusing the materials and harness

The original DSAA 3071 repository is a worked example — useful as a reference, but not the right place to start a new course. It contains 13 weeks of Theory-of-Computation-specific content that will only get in the way.

The TDAA-Go starter is the better starting point. It contains the reusable structure — document templates, build commands, the AI-assisted skills that make up the harness — without any single course's weekly content. Reusing the harness is what lets another instructor adopt TDAA without rebuilding the authoring and grading pipeline from scratch.

Next step

Ready to set up the harness on your own machine? Go to the harness setup guide for the 8-step recipe.
